content:
```json
{
  "mechanism_genome": "GROUNDED-RT1: train a single transformer policy over internet-scale demonstrations, robot affordance embeddings, and language-conditioned planning. The policy uses a learned visual-language affordance embedding as a shared latent space, with a dual objective combining RT-1-style demonstration learning and SayCan-style feasibility grounding: policy loss = demonstration reconstruction + language-conditioned reward + affordance feasibility penalty.",
  "niche_genome": "Long-horizon mobile manipulation and multi-task robot control where internet-scale language knowledge and real-world robot data are available.",
  "observation_genome": "The model should improve task success on unseen objects and instructions compared with RT-1 alone, while reducing overfitting to the training distribution by grounding policy decisions in learned affordances and language.",
  "limitation_genome": "Requires access to internet-scale visual-language demonstrations and robot affordance supervision; may still fail on tasks requiring fine-tuned physical interaction or rare object affordances.",
  "delta_genome": "Changes RT-1 from a demonstration-trained robot policy into a grounded transformer that combines RT-1’s large-scale control with SayCan’s affordance feasibility filtering, using learned visual-language affordances as the common planning currency.",
  "claim_genome": "A robot policy trained jointly on internet-scale language, affordance, and robot demonstrations will outperform RT-1 on unseen objects and instructions because it grounds learned behavior in real-world feasibility and language semantics."
}
```
prompt:
You are a senior researcher in Robotics / Embodied AI who understands how scientific ideas evolve.
Below is an important research lineage in this area, where each paper evolves from its predecessors:

**[2022] Do As I Can, Not As I Say: Grounding Language in Robotic Affordances**
Key Contribution: SayCan: grounds LLM high-level planning in robot affordances by multiplying LLM language scores with learned value functions for feasibility.
Target Problem: LLMs can plan but cannot execute in the physical world; robot skills must be grounded in real-world affordances and feasibility.
Core Method: LLM proposes candidate actions; per-skill value function evaluates affordance probability; select action maximizing product of LLM score × value.
Observed Evidence: SayCan achieves 74% task success on long-horizon mobile manipulation; LLM grounding in robot capabilities enables multi-step planning.
Remaining Limitation: Each skill requires separate value function; no end-to-end training; requires pre-defined skill library; no visual language grounding.
Expected Result/Claim: SayCan: grounds LLM high-level planning in robot affordances by multiplying LLM language scores with learned value functions for feasibility.

  ↓

**[2022] RT-1: Robotics Transformer for Real-World Control at Scale**
Key Contribution: RT-1: Transformer-based robot policy trained on 130K demonstrations across 700+ tasks in a fleet of real robots; 97% success on trained tasks.
Target Problem: Skill libraries are hard to scale; need a single large model that can handle diverse tasks without task-specific engineering.
Core Method: EfficientNet visual encoder + TokenLearner + Transformer; text instructions via FiLM conditioning; action tokenization for discrete control.
Observed Evidence: RT-1 achieves 97% on 700+ tasks; 25% generalization to new tasks; 13× better than baseline; real-time 3Hz inference.
Remaining Limitation: No vision-language pretraining; limited to the training distribution of tasks and objects; cannot leverage internet-scale knowledge.
Expected Result/Claim: RT-1: Transformer-based robot policy trained on 130K demonstrations across 700+ tasks in a fleet of real robots; 97% success on trained tasks.

  ^ **[Source-to-new-work change note]** (from: Do As I Can, Not As I Say: Grounding Language in Robo...)
  Change Summary: RT-1 internalizes SayCan's modular planning+skills into a single transformer policy; eliminates separate skill library by learning from 130K demonstrations end-to-end.
  Main Changed Field: Core Method
  Field Changes: Target Problem: source changed; new work newly added; Core Method: source changed; new work newly added; Observed Evidence: source not reused; new work introduced from outside the immediate source; Remaining Limitation: source not reused; new work newly added; Expected Result/Claim: source not reused; new work newly added
  Field Change Summary: local change from the source driven by method; changed method, target problem; not reused observation, limitation, claim; added method, target problem, observation, limitation, claim.
  Main Change: Core Method | source=LLM proposes candidate actions; per-skill value function evaluates affordance probability; select action maximizing product of LLM score × value. | new=EfficientNet visual encoder + TokenLearner + Transformer; text instructions via FiLM conditioning; action tokenization for discrete control.

  ↓

**[2023] RT-2: Vision-Language-Action Models Transfer Web Knowledge to Robotic Control**
Key Contribution: RT-2: fine-tune a large VLM (PaLI-X 55B) on robot data; robot actions as text tokens; transfers web knowledge to physical manipulation.
Target Problem: RT-1 trained only on robot data; cannot generalize to novel objects/concepts not seen in the 130K robot demonstrations.
Core Method: Tokenize robot actions as text strings; jointly fine-tune VLM (PaLI-X) on web data + robot demonstrations; actions generated as next-token prediction.
Observed Evidence: RT-2 achieves emergent semantic generalization: recognizes novel objects, follows abstract instructions; 2× better generalization than RT-1.
Remaining Limitation: 55B VLM parameters expensive for real-time inference; 1-3 Hz control frequency; still requires abundant robot demonstration data.
Expected Result/Claim: RT-2: fine-tune a large VLM (PaLI-X 55B) on robot data; robot actions as text tokens; transfers web knowledge to physical manipulation.

  ^ **[Source-to-new-work change note]** (from: RT-1: Robotics Transformer for Real-World Control at...)
  Change Summary: RT-2 replaces RT-1's task-specific visual encoder with a 55B VLM backbone; actions tokenized as text; transfers internet-scale knowledge to robot control.
  Main Changed Field: Core Method
  Field Changes: Target Problem: source changed; new work newly added; Core Method: source changed; new work newly added; Observed Evidence: source changed; new work changed; Remaining Limitation: source not reused; new work newly added; Expected Result/Claim: source not reused; new work newly added
  Field Change Summary: local change from the source driven by method; changed method, target problem, observation; not reused limitation, claim; added method, target problem, limitation, claim.
  Main Change: Core Method | source=EfficientNet visual encoder + TokenLearner + Transformer; text instructions via FiLM conditioning; action tokenization for discrete control. | new=Tokenize robot actions as text strings; jointly fine-tune VLM (PaLI-X) on web data + robot demonstrations; actions generated as next-token prediction.

  ↓

**[2024] OpenVLA: An Open-Source Vision-Language-Action Model**
Key Contribution: OpenVLA: 7B parameter open-source VLA based on Prismatic VLM; matches RT-2 performance with 7x fewer parameters; fully open-source training pipeline.
Target Problem: RT-2's 55B parameters are inaccessible to the research community; no open-source VLA with reproducible training pipeline.
Core Method: Prismatic VLM (7B) with DINOv2+SigLIP visual encoder; action discretization to 256 bins; fine-tuned on Open-X-Embodiment dataset.
Observed Evidence: OpenVLA matches or exceeds RT-2-X on 29 tasks; 7B parameters enables deployment on single GPU; enables broader VLA research.
Remaining Limitation: Action discretization limits precision for continuous control; 6.4Hz inference; no contact-rich or dexterous manipulation; single arm only.
Expected Result/Claim: OpenVLA: 7B parameter open-source VLA based on Prismatic VLM; matches RT-2 performance with 7x fewer parameters; fully open-source training pipeline.

  ^ **[Source-to-new-work change note]** (from: RT-2: Vision-Language-Action Models Transfer Web Know...)
  Change Summary: OpenVLA radiates RT-2's VLA paradigm to the open-source research community with 7B parameters; same VLM+action-tokenization approach but accessible and reproducible.
  Main Changed Field: Target Problem
  Field Changes: Target Problem: source changed; new work newly added; Core Method: source changed; new work newly added; Observed Evidence: source not reused; new work newly added; Remaining Limitation: source not reused; new work newly added; Expected Result/Claim: source not reused; new work newly added
  Field Change Summary: reuse in a shifted target problem driven by target problem; changed method, target problem; not reused observation, limitation, claim; added method, target problem, observation, limitation, claim.
  Main Change: Target Problem | source=RT-1 trained only on robot data; cannot generalize to novel objects/concepts not seen in the 130K robot demonstrations. | new=RT-2's 55B parameters are inaccessible to the research community; no open-source VLA with reproducible training pipeline.

  ↓

**[2024] π0: A Vision-Language-Action Flow Model for General Robot Control**
Key Contribution: π0 (pi-zero): VLM backbone + flow matching action head replaces discrete tokenization; enables 50Hz continuous control; dexterous bimanual manipulation.
Target Problem: Discrete action tokenization (RT-2, OpenVLA) limits precision; 6Hz control too slow for dexterous manipulation; cannot model multi-modal action distributions.
Core Method: Pre-trained VLM (PaliGemma 3B) + continuous flow matching action head; denoises action trajectory chunk from Gaussian noise; 50Hz inference.
Observed Evidence: π0 succeeds on contact-rich tasks (folding laundry, assembling boxes); 50Hz control enables dexterity; significantly outperforms prior VLAs on manipulation.
Remaining Limitation: Flow matching requires multiple denoising steps (latency at test time); large compute for training; limited to Physical Intelligence's hardware configuration.
Expected Result/Claim: π0 (pi-zero): VLM backbone + flow matching action head replaces discrete tokenization; enables 50Hz continuous control; dexterous bimanual manipulation.

  ^ **[Source-to-new-work change note]** (from: OpenVLA: An Open-Source Vision-Language-Action Model)
  Change Summary: π0 replaces discrete action tokenization with continuous flow matching; fundamentally new action representation paradigm enabling high-frequency dexterous control.
  Main Changed Field: Core Method
  Field Changes: Target Problem: source changed; new work newly added; Core Method: source changed; new work newly added; Observed Evidence: source not reused; new work newly added; Remaining Limitation: source not reused; new work newly added; Expected Result/Claim: source not reused; new work newly added
  Field Change Summary: main-method replacement driven by method; changed method, target problem; not reused observation, limitation, claim; added method, target problem, observation, limitation, claim.
  Main Change: Core Method | source=Prismatic VLM (7B) with DINOv2+SigLIP visual encoder; action discretization to 256 bins; fine-tuned on Open-X-Embodiment dataset. | new=Pre-trained VLM (PaliGemma 3B) + continuous flow matching action head; denoises action trajectory chunk from Gaussian noise; 50Hz inference.

  ↓

**[2026] RoboAlign: Learning Test-Time Reasoning for Language-Action Alignment in Vision Language Action Models**
Key Contribution: Applies reinforcement learning at test time to improve VLA action accuracy; trains a reasoning policy that aligns language instructions with action outputs for π₀-style VLA models; achieves 17.5%, 18.9%, and 106.6% gains over SFT baselines on LIBERO, CALVIN, and real-world environments using <1% additional RL training data.
Target Problem: π₀'s flow matching produces continuous actions but the alignment between language instructions and action outputs is imprecise; supervised fine-tuning approaches yield inconsistent results for language-action alignment.
Core Method: Sample action tokens through natural language reasoning; refine with reinforcement learning at test time using less than 1% additional training data beyond SFT; bridges gap between language representations and low-level robotic commands.
Observed Evidence: RoboAlign achieves 17.5%, 18.9%, and 106.6% improvements over SFT baselines on LIBERO, CALVIN, and real-world environments respectively.
Remaining Limitation: RL-based alignment reward requires privileged state information (ground-truth task success) unavailable in open-world deployment; cannot derive alignment signal purely from visual observations.
Expected Result/Claim: Applies reinforcement learning at test time to improve VLA action accuracy; trains a reasoning policy that aligns language instructions with action outputs for π₀-style VLA models; achieves 17.5%, 18.9%, and 106.6% gains over SFT baselines on LIBERO, CALVIN, and real-world environments using <1% additional RL training data.

  ^ **[Source-to-new-work change note]** (from: π0: A Vision-Language-Action Flow Model for General R...)
  Change Summary: RoboAlign adds RL-based test-time reasoning on top of π₀'s flow-matching VLA; same continuous action framework, adds alignment-focused RL to bridge language-action gap.
  Main Changed Field: Core Method
  Field Changes: Target Problem: source changed; new work newly added; Core Method: source changed; new work newly added; Observed Evidence: source not reused; new work newly added; Remaining Limitation: source not reused; new work newly added; Expected Result/Claim: source not reused; new work newly added
  Field Change Summary: local change from the source driven by method; changed method, target problem; not reused observation, limitation, claim; added method, target problem, observation, limitation, claim.
  Main Change: Core Method | source=Pre-trained VLM (PaliGemma 3B) + continuous flow matching action head; denoises action trajectory chunk from Gaussian noise; 50Hz inference. | new=Sample action tokens through natural language reasoning; refine with reinforcement learning at test time using less than 1% additional training data beyond SFT; bridges gap betw...


The key unresolved question at the frontier of this lineage:
RoboAlign improves VLA alignment via test-time RL but the reward signal requires privileged state information unavailable in open-world deployment. What mechanism would enable self-supervised VLA alignment — deriving alignment rewards purely from visual observations without ground-truth state access?

Based on the lineage history, source-to-new-work changes, and the current frontier question, generate a new research idea as the next evolutionary node.

Return ONLY valid JSON. Do not wrap it in Markdown fences.

  "idea": "<short name of the proposed idea, 5-15 words>",
  "idea_description": "<150-250 words describing the full research idea>",
  "technical_plan": {

  },

    "why_next_step": "<why this idea follows from the provided context>",
    "lineage_risk": "<most likely way this source connection could be wrong>"
  },
  "evaluation_plan": {
